mechanism_narrative: A sustained plateau in the federal funds rate after a prolonged hiking cycle signals peak monetary tightness; the resulting pause reduces near-term discount rate uncertainty and supports positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:Federal funds rate held at 5.25% since June 2006 after 17 consecutive increases
failure_condition: S&P 500 20-day forward returns are negative in more than 50% of observations when FEDFUNDS is unchanged over the prior 3 months during tightening cycles.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate rises by more than 0.3 percentage points from its trailing 12-month low, it signals weakening labor demand that precedes earnings downgrades, leading to negative 60-day S&P 500 returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Unemployment rate at 4.4% as of October 2006 near cyclical lows
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 55% of instances when the UNRATE trigger condition is met, or the rank correlation between UNRATE change and returns is positive and significant (p < 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous month-over-month contraction in real retail sales and industrial production indicates a broadening demand-supply slump that forces equity risk premiums higher, producing negative S&P 500 returns over the following 5 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:Industrial production and retail sales are key coincident indicators of economic activity
failure_condition: In episodes where both RSAFS and INDPRO decline, S&P 500 5-day forward returns are positive more than 50% of the time, or the interaction term coefficient is not negative at the 5% significance level.

mechanism_narrative: In a high-inflation environment, an elevated federal funds rate above the inflation trend reduces real liquidity and compresses valuations; this mechanism generates negative 20-day S&P 500 returns when CPI inflation exceeds 3 percent.
evidence_refs: fact:Federal Reserve held rates at 5.25% in late 2006 with inflation above 3% YoY
failure_condition: When CPIAUCSL YoY exceeds 3%, S&P 500 20-day forward returns following elevated FEDFUNDS are not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.05), or the average return is positive across triggered windows.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp decline in housing starts below 1.5 million units annualized signals an impending drag on construction employment and household wealth that reduces cyclical earnings forecasts, generating negative 60-day S&P 500 returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing starts have fallen sharply from 2005 peaks to 1772 in September 2006
failure_condition: S&P 500 60-day returns are positive in more than 50% of months when HOUST is below 1500, or the rank correlation between HOUST levels and forward returns is positive.

mechanism_narrative: A falling unemployment rate alongside rising payroll employment indicates a tightening labor market that lifts wage costs and squeezes profit margins, producing negative 5-day S&P 500 returns as margin fears reprice equities.
failure_condition: Joint occurrences of low UNRATE and high PAYEMS do not coincide with negative 5-day S&P 500 returns in more than 50% of cases, or the interaction term is statistically insignificant (p > 0.05) in predicting returns.

mechanism_narrative: Month-over-month industrial production growth above 0.2% signals accelerating manufacturing momentum that supports earnings revisions and drives positive 20-day S&P 500 returns.
failure_condition: When INDPRO MoM exceeds 0.2%, S&P 500 20-day forward returns are negative more than 50% of the time, or the average return is not statistically different from zero (p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: Real GDP growth that outpaces the prior quarter's trend confirms a resilient demand backdrop that supports revenue growth and drives positive 5-day S&P 500 returns upon release.
evidence_refs: fact:Q2 2006 GDP registered solid growth supporting expansion narrative
failure_condition: S&P 500 5-day returns following GDPC1 growth above 2% are negative in more than 50% of observations, or the rank correlation between GDP surprises and returns is negative.
